rationale: This is a canonical example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, featuring the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich phenol (resorcinol) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (zinc chloride) and a Brønsted acid (HCl), leading to the formation of an aryl ketone (2,4-dihydroxyacetophenone) after hydrolysis of the intermediate ketimine salt. The reactants and product are simple, acyclic where possible, and clearly illustrate the core transformation of this named reaction.
collision_avoidance_note: The Houben-Hoesch reaction is a specific type of electrophilic aromatic substitution related to Friedel-Crafts acylation but uses a nitrile and an acid catalyst. This example uses a simple nitrile (acetonitrile) and a highly activated phenol (resorcinol) to distinguish it from general Friedel-Crafts reactions which typically use acyl halides or anhydrides. The use of a simple, non-fused aromatic ring avoids overlap with reactions involving polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons. The nitrile functionality is key to defining this reaction family.
